133.reason: [When a single '&' is encountered, an error 'a' is added, but tokenType is still set to AND, which may cause incorrect token output. The token should not be added to the tokens list when an error occurs.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 0.5 , reason:  [When a single '&' is encountered, an error 'a' is added, but tokenType is still set to AND, which may cause incorrect token output. The token should not be added to the tokens list when an error occurs.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 0.5 , reason:  [When a single '&' is encountered, an error 'a' is added, but tokenType is still set to AND, which will cause the token to be added to the tokens list even though it's an error. The program should not output tokens when there are errors, but the current logic adds the token before checking errors, leading to incorrect output.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  Unconditionally assigns AND token type even when the second '&' is missing (error case). It should only be assigned when the pair is valid.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.5 , reason:  [When a single '&' is encountered, an error is correctly added, but tokenType is still set to AND, which will create an invalid token instead of skipping it. This leads to incorrect output in lexer.txt even when an error exists.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.5 , reason:  [When a single '&' is encountered, an error is correctly added, but tokenType is still set to AND, which causes invalid token output instead of halting or skipping]

144.reason: [Same issue: when a single '|' is encountered, an error 'a' is added, but tokenType is still set to OR, leading to incorrect token output. The token should not be added to the tokens list when an error occurs.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 0.5 , reason:  [Same issue: when a single '|' is encountered, an error 'a' is added, but tokenType is still set to OR, leading to incorrect token output. The token should not be added to the tokens list when an error occurs.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Same issue as above: when a single '|' is encountered, an error is added but tokenType is set to OR, causing the token to be added to the list. This should not happen when there is an error.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  Unconditionally assigns OR token type even when the second '|' is missing (error case). It should only be assigned when the pair is valid.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.5 , reason:  [Same issue as above: for a single '|', an error is recorded but tokenType is still set to OR, causing an invalid token to be added to tokens list.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Same issue as above: for a single '|', an error is recorded but tokenType is still set to OR, leading to incorrect token generation instead of treating it as a pure error]

233.reason: [Error: Format should be "%d %c" to output line number first and error category code, with a space separator. The literal "123" is incorrect.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 1.0 , reason:  [Error: Format should be "%d %c" to output line number first and error category code, with a space separator. The literal "123" is incorrect.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 1.0 , reason:  The output format does not match the required "error line number error category code" (with a space). Instead, it incorrectly inserts "123" between the error type and line number, and omits the space.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 1.0 , reason:  The format string "%c123%d" is incorrect and does not match the required output format "error line number error category code". Additionally, the arguments are swapped; it should print the line number first, then the error type, and include a newline character.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  Incorrect output format. It prints the error type first, inserts a hardcoded "123", lacks the required space separator, and misses a newline. The expected format is "line number error category code".

166.reason: reader.read() consumes the character after '*' unconditionally. If it is not '/', that character is permanently lost and will not be processed in the next loop iteration.

reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  reader.read() consumes the character after '*' unconditionally. If it is not '/', that character is permanently lost and will not be processed in the next loop iteration.
